segments.text: Good morning everyone and thank you for being here.
My name is Savario Savillo, I'm an Italian solution architect at Autodesk and today we'll
be talking about how by leveraging digital twins NAI we can have decision intelligence
as a tool that can be used to enhance our operational decisions and drive return of investment
in maintenance operations.
Did you know the industrial companies lose billions of dollars every year because machines
stop unexpectedly?
Well it turns out that real problem is not in data or the machines, but rather in the
decisions made around them.
Today I want to show how digital twins can evolve from monitoring systems into decisions
and intelligence engines.
In particular we look at how operational context from Autodesk tandem and enterprise data
can be combined with agentic AI reasoning to support better operational decisions.
We look how by combining AI driven decisions value gets compounded to prevent failure or
downtime costs, increasing the return of investments.
In the manufacturing industry and plant failure represents a massive economic impact.
Studies estimate that more than 50 billion dollars are lost every year due to unexpected
downtime.
At the same time poor maintenance practices can reduce productive capacity by 5 to 20%.
Let's say what's interesting is the opportunity that can be disclosed from this.
Research shows that improving maintenance strategies can reduce maintenance costs by 17 to 20%.
And productive maintenance with AI can reduce downtime by 20 to 40%.
So the question is not whether maintenance optimization matters, but rather how can we
make better operational decisions leveraging AI?
To make things more concrete let's consider as scenario.
In a production line a machine maintenance is postponed because it still seems to be working.
At some point the asset fails and the line stops so the maintenance team has to intervene
urgently.
Now we have downtime costs plus emergency repair costs.
This is actually a very common situation in industrial environments.
Reactive decisions lead to failures, failures lead to emergency maintenance and value is lost
along the way.
Therefore a question rises.
What if digital twins could help us reason about these decisions earlier?
What twins are very good at providing insights that can show us performance indicators, but
having insights is not the same as making decisions.
Operational teams still have to answer questions like should we intervene now?
Can we defer maintenance?
What level of risk is acceptable?
If digital twins could support this reasoning process the value could be significant.
Or could it reduce downtime, avoid unnecessary maintenance, optimize interventions and ultimately
preserve operational margins?
To enable this kind of reasoning context is essential and this is where auto-desktan them
plays an important role.
Digital twins consolidate information about assets, their relationships, their location
and their operational context.
They integrate with systems, connect with device data and provide dashboards with operational
metrics.
All this information forms a structured operational context and this is exactly what a decision
engine needs in order to reason effectively.
So the digital twin becomes the data foundation for operational intelligence.
If we add a reasoning layer on top of this context, digital twins can start acting as decisions
engines.
In this model a user expresses an operational intention like should we maintain this asset
now?
This can then reason above the problem a dim boke different tools.
Those tools retrieve enterprise context like asset data, maintenance history or operational
metrics.
The system combines these inputs and returns a context and way recommendation by reasoning
on enterprise rules.
Let's see how such a system can be structured.
On the left we have enterprise context data coming from auto-deskt forma, tandem, documents,
external systems, devices and databases.
All context data is exposed to agent-collable tools using modern protocols as MCP.
In the middle an agentechalcastation layer coordinates multiple agents, the reason above
the problem provided by the user and interact with these tools.
On the right we add the decision components like failure probability estimation, maintenance
simulations and value at risk calculations.
These are the components the reasoning operational recommendations to the user interface.
Let me show now how I should a short proof of concept of our approach with these decisions
engines.
Let's look how decision intelligence can be applied once the data context and custom
KPIs are set up.
Asset managers can look at high-level indicators to have an overview of all asset conditions,
understand the health level and how many operations have been done in a period.
By asking the operation assistant, assets can be ranked based on their remaining useful
life or number operations pending, so that more critical assets in terms of usage and
operations pending can be found rapidly.
By meeting custom economic indicators, assets can be ranked based on their repair or deliverance
and cost.
Advanced KPIs can be built to define the assets value at risk or risk score.
By asking the operational assistant, assets can then be ranked based on their risk score,
so that the user can decide if it's the case of investigating a particular assets conditions.
By looking at specific assets values like failure probability or value at risk, the user
can decide if it's the case to execute a maintenance strategy or defer and set up monitoring
conditions.
The operational assistant can provide a recommendation by looking at the assets parameters based on their
values and benchmarking with past decisions.
When a maintenance decision is to be made, alternatives can be chosen by looking at late
or forecasted tasks.
Each task has a cost and a failure probability reduction rate so that net savings and
return of investment can be simulated.
The operational assistant can compare each strategy to find the one with the highest ROI
giving a final recommendation to the user.
As the maintenance strategy is chosen, a maintenance ticket can be created by…
As operational strategies are done over time, the user can check on their actual effectiveness
by comparing the expected results with their actual value.
That way, strategies can be adjusted so that the operational assistant can improve its
performance over time.
Also, we've seen how by transforming digital twins into decisions and genes, we unlock
additional capabilities to create more value in our operations.
Digital twins and deathlords are great for visibility as they show the current state of assets
and systems, but the interpretation of that information is still left to humans to estimate
risks and decide trade-offs.
Agentic reasoning adds another layer as it can evaluate context, simulate economic
impact and apply decision guardrails.
This leads to better quality decisions whose value compounds over time as a maintenance
decisions made today influences costs, risk and performance tomorrow.
Therefore, Agentic reasoning can help in raising the return of investment by improving
the quality and consistency of decisions every day.
So in conclusion, we've seen how this kind of system can generate recommendations that
improve decisions quality over time and drive better operational return of investment.
Digital twins with Autodesk tandem already provide something extremely valuable, a clearly
understanding of our assets and operations have been prevented value loss from fails and
downtime.
The next step is to build on that foundation enabling digital twins to reason over-enter
price data and support better decisions.
This means helping teams act earlier, reduce downtime and continue improve how maintenance
is performed.
And this is exactly where we can start working together applying this approach to your specific
operations.
So let's evolve together from monitoring operations to operational intelligence.
Thank you.
